title: Conversational soft clock: temporal context inference
description:
Why: Characters have no concept of time of day or day of week. Memories feel decontextualized — there is no way to know if something happened in the morning after a hard week or late at night when someone could not sleep. Temporal context makes memories feel like real experiences not just facts.

What: Per-chat soft-clock.yaml state file tracking inferred timeOfDay, dayOfWeek, lastSessionEndedAt. Keyword signal detector (good morning → morning, just got home → evening, can't sleep → late night, happy friday → friday, etc). Morning-after logic: if previous session ended evening/night and new session opens with morning signal, infer a day has passed and update day-of-week. Inject current session context into memory block header so character knows roughly when they are. Enrich all encoded memories with timeContext field.
notes: No LLM needed — pure keyword matching. State stored per chatId. Feeds into all three capture tiers.

title: Memory tier system: fields, loader, promotion/demotion engine
description:
Why: The current flat store treats a one-off mention the same as a deeply recurring theme. Memories need lifecycle management so important things persist and noise falls away naturally.

What: Add tier fields to Entry/IndexEntry (short|long|core|secondary_core), retrievalCount, recitationCount, cycleCount, lastRetrievedTurn. Update loader to always surface core/secondary_core before budget, short/long within budget. Add promotion engine (score >= 5 short→long, score >= 25 long→core) and demotion (not retrieved in 100 turns long→short, 50 turns short→pruned). Cycle counter: 3 promotion-demotion pairs → secondary_core regardless of tier.
notes: Score formula: retrievalCount + (recitationCount × 3). Demotion is time-based not score rollback. Core and secondary_core are never pruned.

title: Recitation detection: increment recitationCount when memory is used
description:
Why: Retrieval (memory surfaced in context) is a weak signal. Recitation (AI actually used the memory in its response) is a strong signal for promotion. Without detecting recitation, the scoring system is half-blind.

What: process-turn response includes surfacedIds (list of entry IDs that were selected for context that turn). Extension checks the AI response text against each surfaced entry summary using Jaccard similarity. If similarity > 0.3, calls PATCH /api/entries/:id/recite to increment recitationCount. Server recalculates score and triggers promotion if threshold crossed.
notes: Jaccard at 0.3 is an approximation — good enough to distinguish clear use from coincidental overlap. surfacedIds needs to be added to process-turn response.

title: Tier 3: ambient detail classifier
description:
Why: Throwaway lines that reveal who someone IS get lost because they carry no emotional weight. 'Priya has a level 400 character', 'I grew up in Texas', 'I cried at the end of MGS3' — these make someone feel known rather than just remembered. This is where the magic lives.

What: After each turn, extract candidate sentences from both user and AI messages. Filter to: sentences under 120 chars, containing first-person markers (I/my/me/we/my), not ending in ?. For each candidate, run a fast binary LLM call: 'does this reveal a stable fact about identity, history, preferences, or relationships?' If yes, extract the fact and save as user_topics (user message) or character_topics (AI message). Fires async — does not block lorebook update.
notes: Small local model (dolphin3:8b) handles this well. Binary output keeps it fast. Candidate filtering is the key — reduces LLM calls dramatically. User messages → user_topics, AI messages → character_topics.

title: Tier 2: sentiment capture on live chat turns
description:
Why: Emotional peaks in conversation (breakthroughs, fights, confessions, crying together) should be captured automatically without the AI needing to self-tag. The sentiment pipeline already works for story ingests — it needs to run on live turns too.

What: process-turn receives recent messages (last 20) from extension. Runs classifyChunks on them (CPU only, no LLM). If any chunk exceeds 0.65 salience threshold, fires analyzeChunk async (does not block lorebook update). Saves EmotionalBeat under character beats directory plus a companion character_topics ledger entry describing the moment. Works in both RP and conversation mode.
notes: Threshold starts at 0.65, tune from there. Classifier is pure keyword/regex so it adds no latency. LLM analyzer fires only when threshold crossed. Game mode excluded for now.

title: Tier 1: time-based session snapshots (30 min)
description:
Why: Long collaborative sessions leave no memory trace when nothing trips an importance signal. A 2.5 hour paper editing session is meaningful context that the current self-tagging model misses entirely.

What: Extension tracks lastSnapshotTime per chatId in localStorage. On each new message, if 30+ minutes have elapsed, sends last 40 messages to new /api/snapshot endpoint. Server runs a single lightweight LLM call: summarize what was discussed, extract user facts and ongoing work, save as character-scope entries. Prompt is distinct from the chat-import digest — framed as ongoing session summary not historical review.
notes: Reuses existing digest infrastructure. 30 min is user-specified. Last 40 messages is a reasonable window — tune if needed.

title: Three-tier ambient memory capture system
description:
Why: The current self-tagging model only captures what the AI explicitly decides to flag — it misses two entire categories of valuable memory. A 2.5-hour paper editing session left no trace because nothing tripped an importance signal. Low-signal throwaway details ('Priya's level 400 character', 'the MGS3 tears') are what make someone feel *known* rather than just remembered, and no current signal catches them.

What needs to be done:

Tier 1 — Temporal snapshots: Periodic memory saves on a cron/timer, independent of emotional weight. Captures long collaborative sessions where content matters but nothing spikes. Simplest to implement — scheduled digest of recent turns into character memory.

Tier 2 — Sentiment-triggered: Emotional peaks flagged and stored automatically. Crying together, breakthroughs, fights, confessions. The sentiment pipeline (analyzer.ts) already scores salience — this tier is about wiring those scores into the memory write path for chat turns, not just story ingests.

Tier 3 — Ambient detail capture: The hard one. Throwaway lines that reveal who someone IS vs what they're doing right now. Needs a lightweight per-turn classifier that asks: 'does this sentence reveal a preference, history fact, or identity marker?' Low-signal, high-value. The difference between a therapist's notes and a best friend's memory. Possible approach: small prompt asking 'does this line reveal something about who this person is?' run against candidate sentences before they're discarded.
notes: User's exact framing: 'that third tier is where the magic lives — the difference between this AI remembers our conversations and this person actually knows me.'

title: Global lorebook bleed: global-scope memories load for all characters
description: The global scope in the sidecar is intended for conventions that apply everywhere, but all global memories are injected for every character regardless of whether they're relevant. This pollutes context for characters that shouldn't see global entries, and there's no mechanism to restrict or opt characters out of specific global entries.
close_reason: Working as designed — global scope is intentionally injected for all characters. No actual bleed occurring.

title: Zombie entries: completed tasks leave empty entry shells in lorebook
description: When memory entries are marked done or deleted in the sidecar, the corresponding lorebook entry content goes empty but the entry shell remains in Marinara's lorebook. These zombie entries clutter the lorebook and waste context budget slots. Completed/deleted entries should be removed from the lorebook or their enabled flag set to false.

title: Lorebook naming: all character lorebooks share identical name
description: Every character gets a lorebook named 'Marinara Extender Memory'. In Marinara's lorebook list this makes them indistinguishable — users can't tell which lorebook belongs to which character. Lorebook name should include the character name or ID so they're identifiable in the UI.

title: Entry naming: no human-parseable scope/descriptor convention
description: Lorebook entries created by the extension have no readable name or consistent naming convention. The comment marker 'marinara-extender-memory-block' is an internal ID, not a label a user would understand. Entries should follow a scope/descriptor pattern so users know what they're looking at if they open the lorebook.

title: User-requested memory save: persist facts/preferences on command
description: Users should be able to ask the AI to 'remember this' mid-conversation and have the extender write it to the appropriate lorebook tier. Examples: 'remember I prefer dark themes', 'save this list', 'remember my character's backstory'. The AI would emit a structured tag or signal, the extension would intercept it, classify the scope (chat vs character vs global), and call POST /api/entries to persist it as a named entry. Scope selection could be heuristic or user-directed via phrasing.
